entry.title: From Prototype to Production with Joule Studio: Recap of Session 4 of Architecting the AE Webinar
entry.content:
The Enterprise Readiness Gap

If you are using Claude Code, Cursor, or any modern agentic development environment, you can build a working agent in hours. It will look impressive. It will handle the use case you designed it for. And it will have almost nothing in common with what an enterprise deployment actually requires.

Getting an AI agent to production in an enterprise context means: aligning business stakeholders, IT, and architecture before a single line of code is written. It means authentication, role-based access control, and single sign-on handled correctly. It means enterprise-grade infrastructure that scales, testing that covers edge cases and evaluates actual agent behavior, audit trails that satisfy compliance requirements, and monitoring that continues long after go-live.

The gap between a prototype and an enterprise-ready agent is not a matter of effort. It is a structural difference in what the two things are built for.

That gap is what I called 'The Enterprise Readiness Gap', and it is precisely what Joule Studio is designed to close.

SAP's positioning is straightforward: Joule Studio is the complete environment to build, run, deploy, manage, and govern agentic AI solutions at enterprise scale.

 

The right to win rests on three capabilities that are difficult to replicate outside of SAP's ecosystem. First, an intent-based development environment that supports everything from natural language vibe coding to full pro-code with your own IDEs and frameworks. Second, business context built directly into both design and runtime: SAP Knowledge Graph, Signavio, LeanIX, Cloud ALM, domain models, and backend systems connected through MCP servers. Third, a trusted governance layer where observability, logging, audit, and lifecycle management are built into the platform, not bolted on after the fact.

Where Joule Studio Sits

Joule Studio is the development pillar inside Joule Work, SAP's new engagement layer. Joule Work provides three surfaces: Conversations, Spaces, and Development. Joule Studio is the Development surface, accessible via browser, desktop, or mobile. The architecture brings together a design-time environment, an SAP-managed runtime, and governance connections to LeanIX, Cloud ALM, and Signavio for agent evaluations, tracing, and performance monitoring.

Three builders are available in the design-time environment today: an Agent Builder for Python-based conversational agents, a Workflow Builder powered by an SAP-n8n partnership, and a CAP App Builder for UI5-based applications. Each produces standard, portable, Git-versionable output, not proprietary artifacts.

Joule Studio Architecture

 For teams who want to work in familiar tooling, VS Code, Claude Code, and Cursor can all connect into the same Joule Studio project. There is no migration, no copy-paste handover. You work in the web editor when you want speed, switch to your preferred IDE when you want depth, and everything stays in sync.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The Six-Phase Lifecycle: From Intent to Production

Julia Kloppenburg walked us through the complete intent-driven development lifecycle, end to end, and then demonstrated it live on a working agent. The six phases represent a structured approach to close enterprise readiness gap.

Phase 1: Intent. Everything starts with a business outcome described in natural language. What needs to change? Who is affected? What does success look like? Joule Studio captures this as a durable problem statement, enriches it with real enterprise context, from Signavio process maps to LeanIX architecture data to SAP Knowledge Graph semantics, and carries it forward through the entire lifecycle. The intent does not get lost in the handover between business and IT. It becomes the thread that connects every subsequent phase.

Phase 2: Product Requirements Document. From the grounded intent, Joule Studio generates a full PRD: problem statement, target users, success measures, requirements grouped by priority, user stories, acceptance criteria, solution architecture, and milestones. What makes this different from a standard requirements exercise is that the requirements reflect how the business actually runs, grounded in real process and architecture data rather than theoretical assumptions. This phase also forces an early decision: activate standard SAP capabilities, configure what already exists, or build something new.

Phase 3: Specification. The PRD becomes an implementation-ready blueprint. Scope, architecture, integrations, security, task breakdown, business object definitions, and system contracts are all defined here. Critically, compliance and policy requirements are embedded at this stage. Safety is built in, not applied after the fact. This is the point where technical choices and integration patterns are locked down, which eliminates the rework loops that typically consume later phases of development.

Phase 4: Code Generation. The build agent generates agents, workflows, and CAP applications directly from the specification, with SAP context integrated throughout: the right domain models, the right integration approach, N8N where orchestration is part of the solution. The full code base is exposed, rewritable, and Git-versionable. Developers own the output. Teams can inspect it, refactor it, and apply their normal engineering discipline to it.

Phase 5: Testing. Joule Studio validates the end-to-end flow, not isolated components. Automated evaluations assess whether the agent behaved as intended, not just whether it ran. A single test report provides coverage, behavior checks, and per-component failure signals. This is the checkpoint that makes the deployment decision a confident one rather than an optimistic one.

Phase 6: Deployment and Operations. The full solution is pushed to GitHub and deployed to SAP-managed runtime. SAP handles provisioning, scaling, monitoring, and day-to-day operations. Cost and usage signals are tracked continuously in production. The AI Agent Hub serves as the central governance control plane: discover agents, manage their lifecycle, observe behavior, trace decisions, and respond to issues. Deployment is not the end of the story. It is where the improvement loop begins.

Julia demonstrated the full lifecycle on a real system, not a recording, using a new hire onboarding agent built entirely in Joule Studio.

The use case is familiar to anyone who has joined a new organisation: navigating tasks, finding documents, completing mandatory training, and getting answers to questions without continuously interrupting HR teams. The agent handles all of that conversationally, surfaces relevant documents on demand, tracks onboarding task completion, and escalates unresolved queries to HR via notification.

The demo started with the intent phase, where Joule Studio generated an intent fit score of 88 percent from a name and a short description. It showed the auto-generated problem statement, goals, and metrics, with the Signavio recruit-to-retire process visible as business context. The PRD contained detailed requirements, user stories, and solution architecture. The specification included MCP integration details and agent behavior definitions. The test suite covered structural, server, and agent tests, all of which passed. The deployment view showed the agent live, connected to SAP SuccessFactors via Agent-to-Agent protocol, running on SAP BTP.

The point of the demo was not the use case itself. It was the coherence of the process: a single environment that carries the intent from natural language through to a deployed, tested, governed production agent, with the business context embedded throughout.

 Key Clarifications

Joule Studio and SAP Build are not the same thing, and Build is not being replaced. SAP Build continues unchanged: customer-managed, supported. Joule Studio is SAP-managed, AI-first, and designed for intent-driven agentic development. 

What you can build: Conversational agents, system-triggered agents, deterministic CAP applications, N8N workflows, and multi-agent systems. The platform is not limited to agentic use cases.

What Comes Next

Session 5 on July 1 turns to the question of how SAP's Innovation Factory approaches architecting and activating AI at the enterprise level with Helmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Augusto Tornaro, and Vishal Mehta to unpack the methodology behind moving from architecture planning to active AI deployment.

If Session 4 answered how Joule Studio works, Session 5 addresses how the broader activation journey is structured across an enterprise engagement.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Recreating Excel‑Like Heat Maps in SAP Analytics Cloud Using Dynamic User‑Driven Thresholds
entry.content:
If you’ve worked with Excel heat maps, you already know how effortless it feels.

Just select your data → apply Conditional Formatting → Color Scales, and instantly you get a beautiful gradient showing highs, lows, and patterns. The colors automatically react to the values simple, intuitive, and powerful.

Excel :- 

But when you try to replicate the same behavior in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), things start to get interesting.

The Challenge in SAC

 In SAC, you can create heat maps and apply conditional formatting using thresholds. However:

Heat map charts rely on a single measure for color intensity
Conditional formatting in tables works with:
      -  Number ranges (static) OR
     -   Comparison to another measure (dynamic)
The real limitation comes when:  You want the gradient to adapt dynamically based on user-defined thresholds, like min/mid/max inputs similar to Excel flexibility.

Out-of-the-box, SAC doesn’t directly provide this level of interactive gradient control in a native heat map.

 So the question becomes:

Can we mimic Excel’s behavior using SAC building blocks?

Yes, and that’s exactly what this solution does.

 Solution Overview

 Instead of relying on the native SAC heat map, the approach shifts to:

          Using a Table + Calculated Measure + Conditional Formatting

This gives us full control over how colors are applied

 Step 1: Design the Data Structure

In the main SAC table:

Measure X → Holds the actual values (the data you want to visualize)
Values are stored at specific intersections (e.g., based on dimensions like Bay, Post, etc.)
Additionally, create 3 input measures:

  • Minimum
  • Midpoint
  • Maximum

These are not part of the main data grid, but stored at separate intersections where users can input values

Step 2: Enable User Input for Thresholds

Users enter:

  •  Minimum threshold
  •  Midpoint threshold
  •  Maximum threshold

This allows complete flexibility; users can dynamically control how the gradient behaves based on their scenario.

Step 3: Create the Logic – “XColorScore”

1) Created a Restricted Measure "RM-X" restricted  to its intersection at a a story level 

2) Created 3 Restricetd Measures one for each Measure restricted to its intersection at a story level

  •  Minimum (RM - Min)
  •  Maximum (RM - Max)
  •  Midpoint (RM - Mid)

3) Create a calculated measure (XColorScore) that:

Compares each Measure X value against Minimum, Midpoint, and Maximum

Example logic (conceptually):

If X <= Minimum → assign lower score
If X ≈ Midpoint → assign medium score
If X >= Maximum → assign higher score
Essentially, you convert your raw values into a color scoring scale.

This follows the SAC best practice of deriving a numeric output to drive conditional formatting

 

Step 4: Apply Conditional Formatting

Now comes the clever part.

In SAC:

  • Select Measure X 
  • Go to Conditional Formatting (Thresholds)
          Choose:  Compare To → Measure 
                            Comparison Measure → XColorScore

Since thresholds in SAC can compare one measure against another, this enables dynamic, data-driven coloring logic instead of static ranges.

 

Step 5: Achieve the Gradient Effect

By defining appropriate ranges in the threshold:

Green → lower values
Yellow → mid-range
Red → higher values
You effectively simulate:

  •  A heat map
  • A gradient scale
  • Based on user inputs


Why This Approach Works

This design leverages three powerful SAC concepts:

Separation of Data & Logic
→ Raw data (X) vs Colour logic (XColorScore)
User-driven input
→ Thresholds are not hardcoded
Measure-to-measure comparison
→ Unlocks dynamic conditional formatting

 

 Key Takeaways

Native SAC heat maps are great, but not always flexible enough
Conditional formatting with measure comparison is the real game changer
By introducing a scoring layer, you can simulate Excel-like gradients
Most importantly:  You give control back to the business user

Closing Thoughts

Moving from Excel to SAC often exposes these small gaps in flexibility.

But with a bit of creative modelling, SAC can go beyond Excel,especially when you combine:

  • Planning input
  •  Calculated measures
  •  Conditional formatting

This solution is a perfect example of that.

entry.title: Provisioning a Solution in SAP Data Accelerator: A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Hi, community!

SAP is introducing SAP Data Accelerator, an SAP-managed service that enables secure access to data from supported SAP systems through authorized partner solutions. It replaces the customer-managed connectivity setup used in supported partner access scenarios with an SAP-operated service layer designed to support enterprise security, regulatory compliance, and operational availability, while helping reduce brittle integrations, simplify operations, and minimize transition effort.

This guide walks you through the end-to-end process of provisioning a new solution in SAP Data Accelerator using SAP for Me. It is intended for administrators and technical users who need to set up and access a new SAP Data Accelerator tenant. By the end of this guide, you will have a fully provisioned solution and the credentials to sign in to it.

Step 1: Open SAP for Me

Open SAP for Me in your browser and navigate to Portfolio & Products > Applications and choose Start Provisioning in the SAP Data Accelerator Hub tile. 

SAP for Me dashboard showing Applications in Products & Portfolio

Step 2: Use the Create Solution Wizard

The Create Solution wizard opens and displays four steps: General Information, Select Applications, Configure Parameters, and Review. The wizard starts at Step 1: General Information. Wait for any loading indicator to finish before proceeding.

Step 3: Enter General Information

Fill in the required information: Name (1), Path (2), and choose Next Step (3).

General Information page

Step 4: Enter Configure Parameters

Fill in the required information: Region, Cloud Connector Auth Data Password, External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", and choose Review.

Step 5: Review the Information

Review the information entered and if everything looks correct, choose Finish.

Step 6: Download the Authentication Data

You will receive a pop-up screen informing you that provisioning has started and that the resources are being provisioned in the selected resource group. Choose the View in Resources button to check the status of your request. While the tenant is in Processing state (1), a View Notifications button (2) becomes available. Use this button to access the Download connection details link (3).

Tenant Notifications screen

Step 7: Connect the Cloud Connector to SAP Data Accelerator 

The authentication data downloaded from your SAP Data Accelerator instance includes the subaccount details required for the setup in Palantir's platform, such as the region, subaccount, login email address, and password.

Subaccount details

Use the information from those details to connect the Cloud Connector to the SAP Data Accelerator subaccount, as described in the SAP Help Portal page Connecting to SAP Data Accelerator. Complete this step within 5 minutes of downloading the authentication data. The activation link expires 1 hour after it is generated.

Step 8: Start Using SAP Data Accelerator 

Once the account has been activated, your SAP Data Accelerator is ready to use (1). You can log in to your SAP Data Accelerator either using your SAP Data Accelerator URL or using the Main URL (2) provided in the Access Information of the SAP Data Accelerator tenant.

Account ready to use

The purpose of this blog post is to guide you step by step through the process of provisioning a solution in SAP Data Accelerator. For a list of the prerequisites and more information, I suggest you visit the SAP Help Portal documentation Provisioning SAP Data Accelerator.

For more information about SAP Data Accelerator, see SAP Data Accelerator product page.

If you liked reading this post, feel free to share it with your colleagues. Also, I invite you to follow my profile and leave a comment or a question. 

All the best,

Eduardo

entry.title: Slides & Recordings from the 13-Part 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series
entry.content:
Dear All, 

So far we have got tremendous support and response to the 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series.  We have got multiple requests for having slides and recordings from the already completed sessions of the 13-Part 'Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise' Webinar Series. 

 
The Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series is a 13-part community learning journey running weekly from June through August 2026. Each session brings SAP architects, product leaders, and customers together to unpack the What, Why, and How of the Autonomous Enterprise, from the Business AI Platform and agent development to data foundations, governance, and the Context System.
 
It is architects learning together, with content evolving based on community feedback. Sessions cover the practical architectural decisions you need to make today to prepare for an agentic world.
 
Join us live or catch up on-demand.  All sessions are complimentary and open for all. You can join us in the future sessions by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html
 
We are publishing the recordings and slides from every session because this series is a shared learning journey, and learning only works if the materials are accessible to everyone, whether you attended live, joined late, or are catching up between sessions. Below are the recordings and slide decks from the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series, updated after each session.
 
Do consider sharing with your colleagues , customers and partners and join us for the future sessions!
 
Session TitleRecordingSlides
1st JunUnpacking the Autonomous Enterprise for ArchitectsWatchSlides
10th JunWhat SAP's Evolved Portfolio Means for ArchitectsWatchSlides
16th JunArchitecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work and Knowledge GraphWatchSlides
26th JunPlaybook for Building Your Own SAP Agents with SAP Joule StudioWatchSlides
1st JulSAP's Innovation Factory for Architecting & Activating AI in SAP LandscapeWatchSlides
8th JulyAn Architects Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management at Enterprise ScaleWatchSlides
14th JulAn Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of ModelsWatchSlides
16th JulThe AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to RealityWatchSlides
22nd JulData Foundations for an Autonomous EnterpriseScheduled-
29th JulFrom Autonomous Domains to Industry AI: One Platform, Two LensesScheduled-
5th AugAI Governance and Security for an Autonomous Enterprise Scheduled-
18th AugRole of an Enterprise Architects in driving Autonomous Enterprise Scheduled-
20th AugThe Human-Agent Orchestrator: Leading, Governing, and Scaling the Autonomous EnterpriseScheduled-
 
You can register for the future sessions using the link: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html
 
Thank you for your continued support and looking forward to amazing participation in the future!
 
Regards,
Gaurish Dessai, Ralph Richter, Jason Luo & Raghu Banda
Global Enterprise Excellence Team at SAP.

entry.title: From Silos to Strategy: What 20 Years of SAP Data Management Taught One Practitioner
entry.content:
Insights from a conversation with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, Principal Development Consultant at SAP and veteran SAP data management practitioner and consultant

When it comes to data management, there’s no shortage of frameworks, tools, and buzzwords competing for attention. But talk to someone who has spent two decades in the trenches of SAP implementations, and the noise falls away quickly. What's left is a set of hard-won principles every data practitioner should internalize before their next project kicks off.

I recently sat down with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, a longtime SAP practitioner who started his career as a C and Visual C++ programmer before an accidental encounter with an SAP customer in Malaysia in the year 2000 that changed his trajectory. Twenty years later, working as a development lead in the data management space in North America, Rakesh has a clear point of view on what separates successful data management implementations from the ones that stumble.

Start with the Customer's Vision—Not the Data

Ask Rakesh what a successful data management implementation looks like, and he doesn't start with tools or even data. He starts with the customer.

"It all starts with the customer's vision and long-term goal. To fulfill that strategy, what capabilities are required? Within those capabilities, what end-to-end processes are running? And when you come to the level of process, that is where you discover what sort of data is flowing between them."

Data is a mandatory ingredient of any successful implementation—but it's not the starting point. Skip business strategy, and you'll spend the rest of the project reworking things you shouldn't have built.

The Silo Mindset Is Still the #1 Killer of Data Programs

Rakesh's most direct warning was about how organizations think about data:

"Most people think about data in a siloed environment. If procurement is there, the procurement team is only looking at what data they want. They're not looking at where the data is coming from, who's consuming it, or whether something is missing that could impact subsequent processes."

The result? Failure that cascades through the entire customer vision. In SAP's world, he emphasized, "we never talk about silos—we always talk end-to-end process." Data governance, lineage, and design decisions must reflect that end-to-end reality.

Governance Must-Haves: Data as a Whole, Lineage, and Footprint

When asked which governance capabilities are non-negotiable, Rakesh was refreshingly clear:

"To me, it is data as a whole—master data and transactional data—along with data lineage and data footprint. That is what is more critical."

If you understand what data each process requires, where it originates, and which processes should receive it, you can pass only the necessary data to only the necessary processes. Overloading downstream processes with unnecessary data is a recipe for confusion and risk. Before diving into cataloging, classification, or business data fabric conversations, make sure your data lineage and footprint story is airtight.

The Real Lesson from a Successful Project: Step Out of the Comfort Zone

Rakesh's proudest project was a retail SAP S/4HANA implementation at a wholesale trade company, spanning sales, procurement, and finance. What made it stand out wasn't the technology, it was the method.

During the Explore phase, workshops ran from 8 a.m. through the afternoon. Rather than passively "taking data in," Rakesh introduced a collaborative flip-chart mechanic: each business domain maintained its own flip chart, and at the end of every workshop day, each domain presented processes, requirements, and gaps. The result was a unified picture of processes and the data flowing between them, giving the architect visibility into cross-topic dependencies.

His larger point:

"As a team member, it becomes our responsibility to step out of the comfort zone and think from the process perspective. Every rework has an impact on timelines. If you spend some time upfront, you reduce rework and unnecessary risks."

Data professionals who stay strictly in the "load and reload" lane will always be part of the problem. The ones who understand process become indispensable.

Get the Right People in the Room — Especially the End Users

Who should be part of the data conversation? Rakesh was emphatic: the end users (that’s you - the data professionals!).

"On the source side from the customer end, it is mostly the end users. The presence of the department head is also required, but it is primarily the end user, because he or she is the one playing around with this data."

The project mentioned earlier succeeded because business users and department heads were pulled in alongside subject matter experts to answer critical questions: Is this the right data? Is this information really mandatory?

Where the project had loopholes — unsurprisingly — was in data profiling and harmonization. Skipping these topics guarantees pain during execution.

Choose the Right Tool for the Right Job

One of the most candid parts of the conversation was about tool selection. Rakesh's favorite feature on the SAP S/4HANA side? The SAP Migration Cockpit.

"Migration Cockpit comes with standard templates in line with the back-end tables or structures. SAP gives you the right template so the data goes right into that particular thing."

But Migration Cockpit isn't the answer to everything. SAP Signavio provides the complete process picture.  And SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is powerful and ever-evolving solution for AI and next-generation data scenarios.

The Foundation Comes Before the Autonomy

As the industry naturally is buzzing about the autonomous enterprise, Rakesh's closing thought was grounded:

"All concepts are good. We have all these beautiful tools. But the only thing required is properly structuring it out."

You can't have an autonomous enterprise without a data foundation. And you can't build that foundation without doing the unglamorous work: aligning to business strategy, breaking silos, enforcing lineage, involving end users, profiling and harmonizing data, and picking the right tool for each job.

Final Thoughts

If there’s one thread running through Rakesh’s twenty years of experience, it’s this: great data management is less about technology and more about awareness, awareness of business goals, of end-to-end processes, of who consumes what data, and which tools truly fit the problem.

For those of us implementing data management solutions, it is about having the discipline to slow down at the start, ask the right questions, and bring in the right people in order to build a data foundation that will actually support what comes next — whether that’s an SAP S/4HANA migration or the autonomous enterprise of tomorrow.

What’s the #1 lesson from your data & analytics/AI implementations? Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June

June was a full month. Three releases shipped back to back, the team delivered across a wide range of areas, and the German SAP user group DSAG kept the conversations grounded with honest feedback and real customer examples at their AK Business Analytics meeting in St. Leon-Rot.

Learn more in my top features video in June 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates and blogs in my community news blog.

 

My top features in June

Use Object Store Sources in Replication Flows

You can now use object store sources directly in replication flows. Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2, Google Cloud Storage, SFTP, HANA Data Lake Files, and Microsoft OneLake are all supported. The data lands in local table files in Datasphere. If your raw data lives in cloud storage, you no longer need a separate ingestion pipeline.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Oracle as a Source in Replication Flows

Oracle is now supported as a source in replication flows, with Initial Only load type. Full snapshot replication from Oracle to your supported targets in Datasphere. Oracle Wallet authentication is also now supported, enabling SSL and certificate-based authentication via mTLS.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Snowflake as a Source in Replication Flows

You can now create Snowflake connections and use them as a source in replication flows, also with Initial Only load type. For non-public Snowflake environments, Cloud Connector is supported as a reverse invoke proxy, routing traffic through a TLS tunnel.

➡️  More information

 

 

Cloud Connector Support for Confluent Cloud Connections

You can now configure Cloud Connector for Confluent Cloud connections. Same principle as Snowflake: Cloud Connector routes traffic through a TLS tunnel so replication flows can reach Confluent Cloud without public endpoints. Requires Cloud Connector to be set up in your landscape.

➡️  More information

 

 

SQL View Transform in Transformation Flows on File

You can now create a SQL view transform inside a transformation flow on file, using Apache Spark SQL. A full SQL editor with auto-complete, drag-and-drop source objects, real-time validation, and data preview. All of this running directly in your file space. This capability was explicitly listed as not supported until this release.

➡️  More information

 

 

Direct Delta Sharing Client Integration for Transformation Flows

Accessing delta share tables in transformation flows, from BDC Foundation Services or Databricks, no longer requires an intermediate step. The integration is now direct, with no intermediate copying, better performance, and reduced storage overhead.

 

 

CLI Extensions: Pause/Resume Replication Flows + File Space Import/Export

Two CLI improvements in one release. You can now pause and resume replication flows and individual replication objects via the command line. File space objects are also now accessible from the CLI: import and export of spaces, modeling objects, tasks, and connectivity. Fewer things that require manual UI interaction for teams running Datasphere through automation.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enhanced Capacity Unit Monitoring

The Capacity Unit Monitoring tool now has a customizable date and time range filter. No longer limited to the current month. A new Spaces tab shows consumption broken down per space, covering Premium Outbound Integration, object store usage, and total capacity unit consumption. Useful for anyone managing multi-space environments.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enable System Notifications ⭐ KP's Pick

You can now configure email notifications for replication flow and task chain completions directly in your user profile settings, for success, failure, or both. No more manual monitor checking to find out if an overnight run completed. Small feature, real impact.

➡️  More information

 

 

YOUTUBE LINK

 
 

DSAG AK Business Analytics — June Meeting in St. Leon-Rot

On June 19 I joined the DSAG AK Business Analytics working group in St. Leon-Rot for their summer session. These meetings have a specific quality that's hard to find elsewhere. The audience is technical, the questions are direct, and nobody is there to be sold to.

The agenda this time was particularly strong:

▶️  SAP Business Data Cloud roadmap including fresh SAPPHIRE announcements (Fabian Hartje)
▶️  SAP Analytics Cloud roadmap update (Matthias Kraemer)
▶️  AI in practice: how Swisscom uses AI in the SAP ecosystem (Tjarko von Lehsten, Viktor Seide)
▶️  BDC Object Store implementation with real-world examples (Stefan Härtelt)
▶️  360° BDC — Data Provisioning, SAC and Planning, Parts I and II (Maria Alejandra Uruena, Sandy Tran, Jie Deng)

Fresh SAPPHIRE updates, live demos, and real customer examples in one room. That's a good day. Thanks to AK speakers Jörg Steinhorst and Stefan Kahle for organizing, and to everyone who showed up and contributed to the discussion.

 

My favorite blogs in June

SAP Analytics Cloud is expanding live data connectivity beyond the classic live models. @david_stocker explains how BigQuery, Snowflake, and Azure SQL are already supported, with SharePoint and more cloud data lakes coming later this year. No replication, no import jobs. Just a live connection via Smart Data Access and virtual tables behind the scenes. The blog also explains how this underpins Seamless Planning. A good orientation read if you haven't looked at live data connectivity yet.
🔗 Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction

@AndreasForsterpublished a hands-on tutorial for vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with SAP Business Application Studio, Python, and an AI coding agent. Instead of writing the PAL/APL code yourself, you describe what you want and let the agent generate it. Jupyter notebooks and all. The example is a time-series forecast, but the setup works for any ML scenario HANA Cloud supports. He also shows how to add current documentation context to keep the generated code accurate.
🔗 Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline

CDC-based replication flows in Datasphere don't use ODP queues at all. @anilkumarauce traces the full internal architecture step by step: changes are captured at database table level using logging tables, triggers, and a staging layer called the Resilient Data Buffer, all the way from an S/4HANA application table into Datasphere. Exactly the kind of under-the-hood knowledge that saves hours when something unexpected happens in production.
🔗 Under the Hood of ABAP CDC in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows

The most complete provisioning reference for SAP Business Data Cloud on SAP Community. @ShreyasriB built out an 8-part step-by-step series covering the full provisioning journey: from the initial BDC Cockpit setup via SAP for Me through Datasphere, SAC, Databricks, HANA Cloud, Intelligent Apps, and Snowflake. The kind of guide you want open in a second browser tab while doing the actual setup.
🔗 Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: Start with Part 1

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • SAP Enterprise Planning: Where AI meets strategy and execution
  • Business Data Architecture: Modeling Layers for Structured Transformations
  • Upcoming Change: Member Limit Enforcement for Data Action Parameters

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere: Replication Flow Setup, Status & Delta Processing
entry.content:
Introduction: 

This technical guide explains how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). It covers the concepts, the end-to-end setup, how deltas are captured, the related jobs and system tables, and what to expect during initial and delta runs.
You’ll learn:
  • How RMS, APE, and CDC work together
  • How to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view
  • How to configure a Replication Flow in Datasphere for Initial only and Initial + Delta loads
  • What artifacts (logging and buffer tables, jobs) are created in S/4HANA and how to monitor them
  • How to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end-to-end
  • SAP Datasphere local table, Internal delta capture columns (Change Date or Change Type).

Basics:

  • Replication Management Service (RMS): Orchestrates change data capture lifecycles, creates and monitors master/subscriber logging tables, and runs real-time jobs for observing and pushing deltas. Manages the end-to-end reliability of triggers and logging.
  • ABAP Pipeline Engine (APE): An ABAP-based runtime used by Datasphere to extract from ABAP sources (S/4HANA tables or CDC-enabled CDS views). Acts as the “consumer” in CDC terms for Replication Flows. Supporting Initial Only, Delta Only & Initial and Delta options.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Uses database triggers to record INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on base tables that back an ABAP CDS view.Captured changes flow from: Base table triggers → Master Logging Table → Subscriber Logging Table → Buffer Table → Replication Flow into Datasphere target.

Prerequisites and Preparation:

Authorizations:
  • ABAP development access to create a CDS view
  • Access to SM37, DHADM, DHCDCMON, DHRDBMON for monitoring
  • Permissions in SAP Datasphere to create and run Replication Flows
System readiness:
  • RFC/ODP or ABAP connection from Datasphere to S/4HANA (as per your Datasphere connection setup)
  • Sufficient background work processes for real-time jobs
SAP S/4HANA RMS job settings, scheudling and operational tuning:

Standard jobs (auto-created/managed, visible in SM37; commonly run under DDIC)

  • /1DH/CDC_HEALTH_CHECK: Periodic CDC health verification.
  • SAP_DHCDC_DISPATCHER: Dispatcher for CDC orchestration.
  • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB: Moves changes from Master Logging Table to Subscriber Logging Table. Default reschedule interval ~1 hour.
  • /1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA (aka /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA): Joins subscriber logs with base table and writes Buffer Table. Default reschedule interval ~6 hours.

Tuning guidance:

  • Start with defaults, then shorten intervals if your delta SLAs require it and your system resources allow.
  • Coordinate with Basis to adjust job class/priority and periodicity to avoid long-run job lists and ensure timely delta movement.
  • Monitor DHCDCMON (log table depth) and DHRDBMON (buffer latency). If backlogs build, increase job frequency and/or background work processes.

Connectivity:

  • ABAP or SAP S/4HANA Connection between SAP Datasphere and SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Datasphere + SAP S/4HANA: Your Guide to Seamless Data Integration.
  • SAP Cloud Connector for SAP S/4HANA (Private/ On-prem).
Design recommendations: Prefer SAP standard released CDS views or SAP data products where possible for lifecycle stability. If not then create custom ABAP CDS view/entity, data products instead of using direct ABAP tables as context information is lost and significant effort is required to reverse-engineer the logic. 
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

1. Create a CDC‑Enabled Custom CDS View

The example below shows a simplified custom ABAP CDS view Z_SALESORDER on top of a custom base table ZVBAK. It enables analytical usage and data extraction, and configures CDC mapping so deltas are captured.

Sample reference code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZISDSALESORDER'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@EndUserText.label: 'Z Sales Order'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel: {
   compositionRoot:   true,
   representativeKey: 'SalesOrder',
   usageType: {
     dataClass:      #TRANSACTIONAL,
     serviceQuality: #B,
     sizeCategory:   #L
   },
   supportedCapabilities: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION, #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET, #SQL_DATA_SOURCE, #CDS_MODELING_DATA_SOURCE, #EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE ],
   modelingPattern: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION ]
}
@ObjectModel.sapObjectNodeType.name: 'SalesOrder'

@Analytics: {
    dataExtraction: {
        enabled: true,
        delta.changeDataCapture: {
            mapping:[
                {
                    table: 'zvbak', role: #MAIN,
                    viewElement: ['SalesOrder'],
                    tableElement: ['vbeln']  }
            ]
        }
    }
 }
define view Z_SALESORDER as select from zvbak
//Association
  association [0..*]...{<Columns>};

For testing, I developed a custom ABAP program to perform CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on the ZVBAK custom table and validate end‑to‑end propagation. I initially inserted 10 records.

Use transaction SE16N on the CDS SQL view ZISDSALESORDER to view the record count.

2. Create a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere.

Replication Flows are the recommended mechanism for high‑volume replication into SAP Datasphere and supported targets.

2.1 Note: If you choose Initial Only mode, the target will have the same columns as the source (130 columns in this case).

2.2 When using the Initial and Delta mode, SAP Datasphere adds two internal columns (Change_Type and Change_Date) to the Z_SALESORDER local table. The following Change_Type values are typically observed in ABAP CDS replication. Replication Flow Scenarios with Different Load Types.

OperationChange_Type
Initial Load L
Pure Insert I
Delta Load (UPSERT: insert if not exists, else update by key )A 
Delta Load (DELETE)D 

Note: The delta capture table is an internal SAP Datasphere object whose structure may change at any time and is not released for external access.It is only used by SAP Datasphere internal applications (for example, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows etc.)Using the internal delta capture columns (Change_Date or Change_Type) or their content for any external delta replication outside Premium Outbound Integration is not permitted. 

3725855 - Delta Columns CHANGE_TYPE and CHANGE_DATE not visible in Data Viewer or Data Products for Local Tables in HDLF Spaces in SAP Datasphere

3634750 - Unexpected behavior with delta enabled Datasphere local table and models using the same feature

2.3  The Replication Flow’s delta frequency is configured for every 5 minutes (default: 1 hour). You can modify this as required. Using 0 hours and 0 minutes switches to near real‑time.

 

3. Execute the Replication Flow for the first time to perform the Initial Load

3.1 In DHADM, you can see that a new subscriber and buffer table are created in SAP S/4HANA for CDS Z_SALESORDER (check via T‑Codes DHADM and DHRDBMON). This occurs when you run the Replication Flow for the first time. Subscriber ID: C30227C32B841FE19EF81806E972D0BE; Producer Type: CDC; Consumer Type: APE (ABAP Pipeline Engine).

3.2 Master Loggining Table /1DH/ML000000024 and Subscriber Logging table /1DH/SL000000024 got created and can also be monitored in the Transaction DHCDCMON.

Master Logging Table: /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>, It captures delta records from triggers as the single source of truth.

  • Structure: Includes the base table’s key fields and a sequence key (/1DH/SEQUENCE).
  • Quantity: One Master Logging Table per CDC mapping in the CDS view. For example, two CDC mappings generate two Master Logging Tables.

Subscriber Logging Table: /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>, It is populated by the real‑time job /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB when the Master Logging Table has records and Mirrors the Master Logging Table but uses the base table’s key fields.

3.3 Resilient Data Buffer Table: /1DH/RDB0000HK: /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>, It is a transient staging framework / buffer table in the ABAP system that acts as a temporary staging area between S/4HANA and SAP Datasphere, ensuring deltas are safely staged, acknowledged, and only deleted after successful delivery. You can monitor them via transactions DHRDBMON (S/4HANA) or LTRDBMON (DMIS-based systems).

  • For each CDS view + replication flow combination, the ABAP Pipeline Engine/APRS creates one or more RDB tables as buffer objects.  One CDS view + one replication flow → one RDB table. Same CDS view + second replication flow → another RDB table for that flow.
  • The final structure matching the CDS view; populated by the real‑time job /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA and Mirrors the CDS view fields; /1DH/SEQUENCE serves as the key.
  • /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA joins the Subscriber Logging Table with the base table to fill the Buffer Table, ensuring only delta records are forwarded to the target.

3.4 As shown below, the Initial Load has completed, and the Replication Flow is retrying deltas every 5 minutes as configured.

 

3.5 In SAP Datasphere, preview the Z_SALESORDER table; the Change_Type = 'L' after initial load is completed and Change_Date was updated when the records were inserted.

 

4. Execute the Replication Flow: Delta Load with INSERT, UPDATE, MODIFY and DELETE Scenarios

4.1 Using the ABAP program, INSERT 2 new sales orders into ZVBAK. In SE16N (ZISDSALESORDER), you’ll see 12 records, and DHRDBMON shows 2 new entries as Current records.

 

 

Buffer Table /1DH/RDB0000HK-/1DH/OPERATION value is U means “UPSERT” (used for both INSERT and UPDATE operations in the RDB buffer)

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load frequency interval elapses.

You’ll notice that the two new orders (5011, 5012) have Change_Type = “A” (upsert: insert if not present, or update by primary key if it exists), whereas “L” denotes the initial load (records loaded during the first full load).

 

4.2 UPDATE 3 existing sales order: 5004, 5005,5006.

You’ll notice that for orders 5004, 5005, and 5006, Change_Type changed from “L” (Initial Load) to “A” (Upsert).

4.3 DELETE three existing orders: 5006, 5007, and 5008.

In the buffer table /1DH/RDB0000HK, the /1DH/OPERATION value is “D” for DELETE operations

In the SAP Datasphere local table Z_SALESORDER, deleted records appear with Change_Type = “D.” On the source side (SAP S/4HANA table ZVBAK and the CDS view Z_SALESORDER), those records were removed from the database. This confirms that Replication Flows with CDC in SAP Datasphere capture all changes, CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE.

5.High‑Level Flow for ABAP CDS View Replication into SAP Datasphere

On the first execution of a Replication Flow, the system creates the subscriber tables, RDB buffer tables, and logging tables. These tables are generated per object + replication flow combination.

The Initial Load transfers data from the source CDS view to SAP Datasphere, and the corresponding Change_Type = Lin the Datasphere local table.

For delta loads, CDC database triggers capture CRUD changes on the base table and write them to the Master Logging Table.

  • Master Logging Table: Stores delta records with /1DH/SEQUENCE as the key.
  • Observer Job ( /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB ) Moves entries from the Master Logging Table to the Subscriber Logging Table.
  • Subscriber Logging Table: Holds delta records keyed by the base table’s keys.
  • Push Delta Job ( /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA ) Joins subscriber logs with the base table to prepare records for extraction.
  • Buffer Table: Provides the final CDS-aligned structure for Datasphere to ingest into the target defined in the Replication Flow.

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load interval has elapsed.

After the initial load, for delta processing:

  • Create/Update operations are represented as Change_Type = A
  • Delete operations are represented as Change_Type = D

 

Conclusion:

Replication Flows with CDC provide a reliable way to move CDS-based data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere with clear handling for initial loads, updates, inserts, and deletes. During the first run, the full dataset is loaded into the target, and during delta processing the runtime automatically captures source changes through triggers, logging tables, and the resilient data buffer before delivering them to Datasphere. In the target local table, the Change_Type values help validate the lifecycle of each record: L for initial load, A for upsert-style delta changes, and D for deletions. This makes it easier to monitor data movement end to end and confirm that the source and target remain synchronized.

 

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE 
  2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/replication-flow-blog-series-part-1-overvi... 
  3. https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/datasphere/best-practices-troubleshooting 
  4. https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4428871866.html 

Happy Learning!😊

entry.title: Architecture Deep-Dive: Transforming SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Architecture Deep-Dive: Transforming SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud

As organizations navigate complex enterprise modernization, the pressure to transition legacy data warehouses into agile frameworks has intensified. However, completely tearing down years—or decades—of structural investments in SAP BW is rarely a viable or cost-effective option.

The strategic alternative is a phased modernization journey leveraging SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) to implement a comprehensive Business Data Fabric & Data Mesh Approach. By adopting the Optimize, Modernize, and Innovate framework, enterprise architects can systematically evolve an existing SAP BW system into an agile, AI-driven data fabric without disrupting live operations.

📊Modernization Blueprint: The Optimize-Modernize-Innovate Journey

Part I: The Sequential Core Technical Pillars

Phase 1: Optimize – Reducing Footprints & Preparing the Landscape

The journey begins by stabilizing the current warehouse estate and tackling infrastructure overhead:

  • Step 1.1: Data Offloading: Cold, non-critical, or historical data that is rarely accessed for active operational reporting is offloaded from the transactional environment directly into the Object Store (SAP HANA Data Lake Files). This action effectively decouples storage capacity from active compute resources, dramatically optimizing infrastructure run costs.

  • Step 1.2: System Move (As-is): Existing installations of SAP BW NetWeaver 7.5 or SAP BW/4HANA are migrated "as-is" into 2a. SAP BW, private cloud edition. This move preserves data structures, custom logic, and transformations without demanding an immediate, high-risk reconstruction of legacy assets.

Phase 2: Modernize – Shifting to a Business Data Fabric

Once optimized, data structures transition from isolated warehouse tables into accessible enterprise data products:

  • Step 2.1: Create/Activate Data Subscription: Utilizing the native Data Product Generator tool, architects create data subscriptions to extract info from legacy BW InfoProviders into dedicated Local Tables within an isolated SAP BW Space inside 2b. SAP Datasphere.

  • Step 2.2: Run Merge Task: Incremental data updates land inside an inbound delta buffer. A scheduled Merge Task (orchestrated via an SAP Datasphere task chain and triggered by SAP BW process chains) processes these delta components, continuously applying changes back into the local tables.

  • Step 2.3: Scenario Generator (Complex Modeling): For intricate, multi-layered historical models, the Scenario Generator helps automatically resolve and mass-generate multiple data subscriptions while preserving strict semantic dependencies.

  • Step 2.4: Create Custom Data Product: Using the Data Sharing Cockpit, raw technical data models are contextualized and exposed as governed Custom Data Products listed inside the central 2e. Catalog & Marketplace.

Phase 3: Innovate – Zero-Copy Advanced AI & Closed-Loop Analytics

The final phase leverages this modernized fabric to power advanced business intelligence and machine learning pipelines:

  • Step 3.1: Share Data Product: Rather than creating redundant physical copies of massive data sets, data products from the Marketplace are securely shared instantly with 2d. SAP Databricks via Zero-copy data sharing.

  • Step 3.2: Advanced AI/ML in Databricks: Data lands in the Databricks Unity Catalog as shared delta tables, where data scientists use ML Notebooks for advanced data engineering and predictive forecasting.

  • Step 3.3: Publish New Data Product: The downstream intelligence generated inside Databricks is packaged using the SAP Business Data Cloud SDK and published straight back into the central Marketplace as an enriched data product.

  • Step 3.4: Integrate Other Data Sources: New, cloud-native data flows directly into 2b. SAP Datasphere, utilizing out-of-the-box, SAP-managed data products and pre-built intelligent content from SAP S/4HANA or SAP SuccessFactors to support clean core principles.

  • Step 3.5: Combine Data & Model: Create views and analytic models within SAP Datasphere to combine historic BW data (via custom data products) with new SAP-managed data products for holistic views.

  • Step 3.6: Reporting & Analysis in SAC: Finely tuned analytical models are exposed directly to 2c. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for unified corporate reporting and real-time insights.

  • Step 3.7: Seamless Planning Data Entry: SAC dashboard planners can execute data entries that write planning logic and allocations natively back into the underlying Datasphere local tables, enabling closed-loop hybrid planning.

Part II: Real-World Architectural Scenarios

The transformation framework natively accommodates four distinct deployment methodologies depending on the lifecycle of specific functional areas:

  • 🏢Scenario A: Preserve Historic Data & Replace BW (Yellow Path): Historic BW InfoProviders are pushed via the Data Product Generator into custom data products. In parallel, new analytical pipelines are established directly from operational sources. Both streams are blended seamlessly within Datasphere views and surface in SAC. This allows the structured, gradual decommissioning of the legacy on-premises SAP BW core system over time without losing access to historical data.

  • 🌿Scenario B: Greenfield Approach (New Sources, Clean Core) (Green Path): For entirely new implementations or fresh source systems (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors or cloud-native SAP S/4HANA instances), the framework skips the private cloud edition entirely. Data flows directly through out-of-the-box, SAP-managed data products and pre-built intelligent content inside BDC.

  • 📐Scenario C: Extended Greenfield Scenario (Cyan Path): When SAP-managed data products do not provide the entirety of necessary bespoke business logic, developers create custom semantic layers directly within Datasphere views and Analytic Models. Traditional staging tiers are fully replaced with native cloud views.

  • 📊Scenario 😧 Seamless Planning (Hybrid) (Magenta Path): This pathway connects planning loops between the analytical surface and the data fabric. Dashboard allocations made in SAC write back natively to Datasphere local tables. Furthermore, existing legacy BPC planning data models can be ingested and combined into these models.

Part III: Commercial Prerequisite Deep-Dive

Beyond the purely technical merits, this architecture addresses the long-standing problem of rigid software licensing. Under the SAP Business Data Cloud framework, all underlying services fall under a unified cloud contract driven by Capacity Units (CUs). Organizations can dynamically allocate and shift these units across the entire stack in self-service mode.

However, when architecting your BDC deployment blueprint, you must factor in the strict minimum baseline requirement enforced by the platform:

  • Core Tenant Provisioning Requirement: Enforces a strict hard floor minimum of 1,532 Capacity Units per month explicitly to provision and spin up a base tenant of SAP Datasphere (providing the baseline compute and disk storage resources required to run the environment).

If you offload massive volumes of data to the Object Store and downsize your active private cloud BW footprint, those exact Capacity Units are immediately freed up. You can instantly pivot those units to power your high-compute Databricks AI modeling nodes or expand user seating inside SAP Analytics Cloud—all without changing your overarching subscription framework.

References

https://help.sap.com/docs/business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud

https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-sap-business-data-cloud?searchId=99475d07-220d-49ea-a5ad-82bbf9972c67&listPosition=1

#SAP #SAPDatasphere #SAPAnalyticsCloud #SAPBDC #SAPBTP #SAPBW #BW4HANA #CloudMigration #Databricks #DataEngineering #AI #MachineLearning

entry.title: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Webinar Series - two sessions this week on 14th and 16th July
entry.content:
We have Two sessions coming up this week in the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series, both directly relevant for anyone making architectural decisions around AI. 
 
Session 7 - Monday 14 July | 17:00 CET / 11:00 ET / 08:00 PT An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of Models With Markus Kohler, Simon Knehr , and Ashok Veilumuthu
SAP is the only company that has trained AI models on the full depth of its institutional knowledge — decades of business process expertise, application code, financial outcomes, and industry best practices. This session explores what the SAP Family of Models is, how business context delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs, and what it means for architects evaluating AI model strategies. If you are deciding between generic foundation models and purpose-built enterprise models — or trying to explain the difference to your leadership — this is the session.
 
Session 8 - Wednesday 16 July | 14:00 CET / 08:00 ET / 05:00 PT -  The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
 
Every architect asks: what does the target architecture look like, and how do I get there? This session answers both - covering the AI-Native North Star Architecture (how AI, data, applications, and agents converge as a unified system), the AI Golden Path (a guided decision framework from idea to production) and the Architecture Center in General. Includes live demonstrations and worked examples showing how reference patterns translate into real implementation decisions. If you have been looking at the published assets on the SAP Architecture Center and wondering how to apply them, this is where it comes together.
Register once, attend any session: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

You can download and import the updated calendar file to ensure all remaining sessions are added to ...

 Slides and recordings from previous session here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/slides-amp-recordings-from-the-13-part-arc...

Feel free to share this with to anyone in your architecture, AI, or technology leadership teams who would benefit.

entry.title: Your SAP Commerce Cloud Data, On Your Terms: Introducing SQL View Gateway
entry.content:
If you've ever needed to get data out of SAP Commerce Cloud for a downstream integration, a report, or a custom data pipeline, you already know the challenge. The data is there — orders, customers, products, transactions — but getting it out reliably, securely, and without building something from scratch has not always been straightforward.

SQL View Gateway, available in SAP Commerce Cloud since April 2026, is a new answer to that challenge.

How teams have been solving this until now

There is no shortage of ways to extract data from SAP Commerce Cloud. Teams today use a range of approaches depending on their context:

  • Custom integrations — partner-built or developed in-house, tailored to specific needs
  • DBSync booster — replicates Commerce data to a secondary database for downstream consumption
  • ImpEx file export — extracts data in structured files for batch processing
  • OData API — exposes Commerce data through a standardized API layer

Each of these is a legitimate solution. They serve different needs, and many customers use them successfully. But they share a common constraint: none of them provide direct, real-time, read-only SQL access to Commerce data without additional infrastructure, custom development, or both. For teams that need exactly that — a direct SQL connection, minimal overhead, and data as it exists right now — there was no supported out-of-the-box option.

SQL View Gateway fills that gap.

What SQL View Gateway is

SQL View Gateway gives you direct, read-only SQL access to your SAP Commerce Cloud data using any standard SQL client, BI tool, or data pipeline you already work with.

There is no file to export, no secondary database to maintain, and no custom API to build. You connect using standard SQL credentials and query the data directly — the same way you would connect to any database.

What data can you access?

SQL View Gateway exposes SAP Commerce data as SQL views. In practice, this means you can query business objects like:

  • Orders and order entries — status, totals, discounts, delivery details
  • Products and catalog data — product codes, names, categories, pricing
  • Customers — account data, registration details
  • Returns and refunds — return requests, refund amounts, statuses
  • Promotions and discounts — applied discounts, vouchers
  • And more — any Commerce type that your team configures for exposure

You decide exactly which types and attributes each consumer can see, through a role configuration defined in your project code. Security-critical data — passwords, tokens, API credentials, access restrictions, and internal framework types — is automatically blocked before it can ever be exposed, regardless of what you configure. The block list is maintained by SAP and applied at the platform level.

It is worth being explicit about one thing: SQL View Gateway does not manage data privacy compliance on your behalf. The data that comes through is raw Commerce data. You are responsible for ensuring that what you expose — and to whom — is compliant with applicable regulations, including data processing obligations, consent requirements , and cross-border data transfer rules. This is no different from any other data integration approach, but it is important to factor in when designing your roles and deciding what to share with downstream systems.

How it works technically

SQL View Gateway runs on a dedicated compute layer that is completely separate from your primary Commerce database. This matters for two reasons:

  • No performance impact on your production system. Queries against the gateway run on their own compute resources. Your store-front transactions are unaffected no matter how heavy the load on the gateway side.
  • Near real-time data. The gateway accesses the same underlying data as your production system without going through a separate replication process. Under normal conditions, the data you query reflects your live Commerce state with millisecond-level latency.

The gateway enforces TLS 1.2 or higher on all connections. Access is read-only by design — it is not possible to write to Commerce data through SQL View Gateway.

From concept to connection: how setup works

Setting up SQL View Gateway involves four steps. Two are handled by developers in the Commerce project, two are handled by administrators in Cloud Portal.

Step 1 — Define roles (developer)
A role specifies which Commerce types and attributes a particular consumer is allowed to see. Roles are defined as configuration files in the data-extraction-configuration directory of your Commerce project — plain text, version-controlled alongside your code.

A role called salesreporting, for example, might expose order totals, statuses, and customer references, but not payment details or personal contact data. You can define as many roles as you need.

Step 2 — Deploy (developer)
Commit the role configuration and trigger a standard Commerce deployment. The system reads the role definitions and creates the corresponding SQL views in the gateway database. No special deployment steps are needed beyond selecting the correct update mode.

Step 3 — Provision a gateway (administrator)
In Cloud Portal, create a gateway for the target environment. You select the compute tier (which determines cost and throughput), configure network access (VPN or public), and specify the IP range of the machines that will be allowed to connect — this is a security measure that restricts access to only the specific systems you designate as consumers. Provisioning takes approximately 20 minutes. You can create up to 20 gateways per environment — useful if different teams or systems need separate connectivity with different performance characteristics.

Step 4 — Create credentials (administrator)
Create a named credential in Cloud Portal, assign it to one of your deployed roles, and the system generates a login and password. Share those credentials with the downstream system or team. Each credential can only access the data scope of its assigned role — nothing more.

That's it. The downstream tool connects via standard SQL using the generated credentials, and queries the views defined by the role.

Cost: what to expect

SQL View Gateway is billed through Consumption Credits — SAP's flexible pay-per-use model. The cost of a gateway depends on its compute tier, measured in Capacity Units per month.

Gateway compute is sized in vCores: 2, 4, 8, up to 32. There is a minimum constraint — a gateway must be at least 25% of your primary database's vCores. A small Commerce environment on 8 primary vCores would need at least a 2 vCore gateway. A larger environment on 32 primary vCores would start at a minimum of 8 vCores.

The right sizing depends on your workload: how many consumers connect simultaneously, how frequently they query, and how complex those queries are. SAP provides a Capacity Estimator and a resource planning guide to help you work through this. A practical approach is to start at the minimum allowed tier and scale up if CPU utilization consistently approaches 80%.

If you do not yet have Consumption Credits, this is a good moment to consider making the switch from your current billing model. Consumption Credits give you fine-grained cost visibility and the flexibility to scale usage up or down without renegotiating your contract.

SQL View Gateway and Business Data Cloud: understanding the difference

SAP offers two complementary solutions for working with Commerce data beyond the store-front: SQL View Gateway and Business Data Cloud. They are not competing products. They solve different problems, and understanding which one to reach for matters.

Business Data Cloud is SAP's strategic platform for enterprise analytics. It brings together data from multiple SAP sources — Commerce, S/4HANA, Ariba, and others — and delivers it as standardized, harmonized data products. These are curated, semantically rich datasets ready for business intelligence reporting, advanced analytics, and machine learning. If your goal is a unified view of your business across your SAP landscape, with the governance and AI capabilities to match, Business Data Cloud is the right tool .

SQL View Gateway works at a different level. It gives you direct access to raw, live Commerce data — exactly as it exists in the database at that moment, structured the way your Commerce platform structures it. There is no aggregation, no harmonization across sources, no semantic model applied. What you get is fast, lightweight, and flexible: a direct SQL connection to your Commerce data with the access controls you define. SQL View Gateway is the right choice when you need that raw connection — to power a custom pipeline, synchronize a specific dataset to an external system, build an operational dashboard on live data, or support a use case where the data model is still evolving and you need the flexibility to work with it directly.

To make it concrete: if a logistics team wants a live view of order statuses and consignment data to monitor SLA compliance, SQL View Gateway is the direct path. If the business wants a cross-brand performance dashboard combining Commerce revenue data with finance data from S/4HANA, Business Data Cloud is the right starting point.

The two solutions serve different purposes and different audiences. Choosing between them is not an either/or decision — your organization may have good reasons to use both, for different use cases.

 SQL View GatewayBusiness Data Cloud
DataRaw, live Commerce dataHarmonized, multi-source data products
LatencyMilliseconds (real-time)Near real-time to batch, depending on pipeline
ScopeSAP Commerce CloudMultiple SAP sources combined
Best forDirect access, custom pipelines, operational dataBI, advanced analytics, ML, AI insights
SetupLightweight, self-service in Cloud PortalBroader platform setup

When in doubt: if your question is "I need to get live Commerce data into my system," SQL View Gateway is likely what you need. If your question is "I need analytics across my SAP landscape," Business Data Cloud is the right starting point.

A few things to plan for

SQL View Gateway is designed to be self-service and straightforward to set up, but a few operational considerations are worth knowing upfront:

  • Hyperscale tier required. SQL View Gateway is only available on the Hyperscale database tier. If you are on a different database tier, it is not currently available. Your SAP account team can confirm your current tier.
  • Gateway availability. SQL View Gateway is not classified as a business-critical service. During maintenance, system initialization, or database backup operations, the gateway enters a temporarily blocked state. Consumers should be designed to handle brief unavailability gracefully rather than treating the connection as always-on.
  • One role per credential. Each credential gives access to exactly one role's data scope. If a downstream system needs data from multiple roles, it will need multiple credentials and connections. This is a deliberate security design — it keeps access auditable and enforces least privilege.
  • All access is logged. Every connection event and every credential management action is recorded in an audit trail. This is useful for compliance and for tracking down unexpected access patterns.

Who should consider SQL View Gateway

SQL View Gateway is most relevant if you recognize any of these situations:

  • You need a live data connection from Commerce to an external system — a data warehouse, an operational dashboard, a custom pipeline — without building a bespoke extract.
  • Your existing data access approach is not the right fit for a specific use case. SQL View Gateway is not a replacement for the other approaches; it is an additional option for scenarios they do not cover well.
  • You are migrating from on-premise Commerce to the cloud. Direct database access was common on-prem. SQL View Gateway is the supported, governed equivalent i n the cloud.
  • You want to connect Commerce data to SAP Datasphere or SAP Analytics Cloud. SQL View Gateway provides a convenient SQL data source for this integration. We will cover this end-to-end in the third post in this series.
  • You need auditable, role-scoped data access. Each connection sees only what its role permits, and every access event is logged.

What you need to get started

SQL View Gateway is available to all SAP Commerce Cloud customers running on the Hyperscale database tier. If you're not sure which tier you're on, your SAP account team can confirm.

You also need Consumption Credits. Once both prerequisites are in place, provisioning a gateway is a self-service operation in Cloud Portal — no support ticket required.

What's next in this series

This post covers the what and the why. The next two posts go deeper:

  • Post 2 — Technical deep-dive: Architecture, role configuration in detail, gateway provisioning, credential management, connectivity options, and cost estimation walk-through.
  • Post 3 — Integration guide: Step-by-step walk-through of connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud using SQL View Gateway.

Learn more

  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • Roles and data scopes guide video
  • Resource planning and estimating guide video
  • Capacity Estimator documentation
  • Data extraction documentation
  • SQL View Gateway documentation 

Have questions or want to share how you're planning to use SQL View Gateway? Drop a comment below.

entry.title: SQL View Gateway: A Technical Guide to Setup and Configuration
entry.content:
SQL View Gateway: A Technical Guide to Setup and Configuration

The first post in this series covered what SQL View Gateway is and why it exists. This post goes deeper: how the pieces fit together, and how to configure everything end to end.

Setting up SQL View Gateway involves four steps. Two are done by a developer in the Commerce project. Two are done by an administrator in Cloud Portal. They don't need to happen at the same time, and Steps 1–2 and Step 3 can proceed independently — an administrator can provision a gateway before any roles are deployed. The only hard dependency is at the last step: a credential cannot be created without an existing, deployed role to assign to it.

How SQL View Gateway works

Before diving into configuration, it helps to understand what you're actually setting up.

SQL View Gateway runs on a dedicated compute layer that is completely separate from your primary Commerce database. Queries from external tools run on that separate layer — your storefront transactions are unaffected no matter how heavy the load on the gateway side.

The gateway accesses the same underlying data as your production system. Under normal conditions, the data you query reflects your live Commerce state with millisecond-level latency. There is no separate replication process, no scheduled export, no intermediate file.

Access is read-only by design. It is not possible to write to Commerce data through SQL View Gateway. All connections are required to use TLS 1.2 or higher. Standard SQL drivers and clients honor this requirement by default.

The data is exposed as SQL views. Each view corresponds to a Commerce type — orders, products, customers, and so on — with exactly the attributes your configuration exposes. Nothing more.

Step 1: Define roles (developer)

A role specifies which Commerce types and attributes a particular consumer is allowed to see. Roles are the foundation of everything else — a gateway credential without a deployed role has nothing to expose.

Where role configuration lives

Role definitions are stored in .properties files inside a data-extraction-configuration directory within any extension's resources folder. You can spread role configuration across multiple extensions — the system collects all .properties files from all data-extraction-configuration directories it finds and applies the combined result.

How you organize this is up to you. Some teams keep all roles in one file in one extension. Others split by role, by domain, or keep role definitions in the extension that introduces the relevant Commerce types. Subdirectories within data-extraction-configuration are fine too — the system scans recursively, so folder structure doesn't matter. Any structure works — the system doesn't care about file names or how many files there are, only about the properties they contain.

The only requirement: the extensions containing your role configuration must be included in your project via manifest.json or localextensions.xml. No special entry for data-extraction-configuration is needed — standard extension inclusion is sufficient.

This is plain text, version-controlled alongside your code. Treat it like any other configuration — peer review, change history, deployment pipeline.

The properties format

Each role requires a declaration of which types to expose, and optionally a list of attributes for each type:

data-extraction.role-based-config.<roleName>.types=<Type1>,<Type2>
data-extraction.role-based-config.<roleName>.<Type>.attributes=<attr1>,<attr2>,<attr3>

The attribute list is optional. If you declare only the type, the system exposes all attributes of that type that exist in the database and are not on the security block list. This is a convenient shortcut for types where you want broad access, but be deliberate — for types like Customer or User, "all attributes" may include more than you intend.

If you declare attributes without a matching type declaration, they are silently skipped.

A worked example: salesreporting

Say you want to give your reporting team access to order data — totals, statuses, discounts — but not payment details or customer contact information.

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.types=Order,OrderEntry,ReturnRequest,Discount,Product
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Order.attributes=PK,guid,code,creationtime,modifiedtime,currency,date,status,totalPrice,totalTax,totalDiscountsAmount,deliveryCost,net
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.OrderEntry.attributes=PK,creationtime,modifiedtime,entryNumber,order,product,quantity,basePrice,totalPrice,discountValues
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.ReturnRequest.attributes=PK,code,order,creationtime,modifiedtime,status,refundAmount
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Discount.attributes=PK,code,value,type,currency,creationtime
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name,description

This gives the reporting team a focused, minimal view of what they need — without exposing anything they don't.

Localized attributes

Some Commerce types have localized attributes — attributes that hold different values per language, such as Product.name or Product.description. SQL View Gateway handles these differently from regular attributes, and the behavior depends on how you configure them.

Implicit locale — write the attribute name without a language qualifier:

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.types=Order,OrderEntry,ReturnRequest,Discount,Product
data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name,description

The system detects that name and description are localized for Product and automatically fetches them in the default language. The view columns are named name and description — no language suffix. The default language is en, configurable via a system property in local.properties:

data-extraction.view.localized.default-language=en

This is a standard SAP Commerce Cloud system property — not part of the role configuration files.

Explicit locale — append the language in square brackets:

data-extraction.role-based-config.salesreporting.Product.attributes=PK,code,name[en],name[pl],description[en]

Each language produces a separate column with a language suffix: name_en, name_pl, description_en. Use this when downstream consumers need to know which language a column contains, or when you need multiple languages side by side.

All-attributes mode (no attribute list) — localized attributes are treated as implicit locale: fetched in the default language, column named without suffix.

Configuration View columnUse case
name[en]name_enMultiple languages side by side; consumer needs to know the language
namenameSingle language; simpler column contract for consumers
No attribute listnameSame as implicit locale

A few rules to be aware of:

  • If the configured default language does not exist in the Commerce system, the attribute is skipped and a warning is logged — no error, no deployment failure
  • Malformed language qualifiers (name[], name[invalid], name[en][de]) are silently filtered out
  • If an item has no translation for the requested language, the localized column contains NULL — the row is still present in the view. If your query must return only items with a translated value, add an IS NOT NULL condition on the localized column

Role name constraints

Role names are validated against a strict pattern:

  • Must start with a lowercase letter
  • Only lowercase letters, digits, and underscores allowed
